coredata.dc:title: A pH-responsive novel delivery system utilizing carbon quantum dots loaded with PT2385 for targeted inhibition of HIF-2α in the treatment of osteoarthritis

coredata.dc:description:
Background Osteoarthritis (OA) is a progressive joint disorder marked by the degradation of cartilage. Elevated concentrations of hypoxia-inducible factor-2α (HIF-2α) are intricately linked to the pathological development of OA. PT2385 has demonstrated effective inhibition of HIF-2α, thereby potentially impeding the initial advancement of OA. Nevertheless, challenges persist, including limited penetration into the deeper layers of cartilage, issues related to charge rejection, and a heightened rate of clearance from the joint. These constraints necessitate further consideration and exploration.
Methods It has been demonstrated that PT2385 exhibits efficient inhibition of HIF-2α expression, thereby contributing to the delay in the progression of osteoarthritis. The pH-responsive attributes of carbon quantum dots, specifically those employing m-phenylenediamine (m-CQDs) coated with bovine serum albumin (BSA), have been systematically evaluated. In both in vitro settings involving cartilage explants and in vivo experiments, the efficacy of BSA-m-CQDs-PT2385 (BCP) has been confirmed in facilitating the transport of PT2385 to the middle and deep layers of cartilage. Furthermore, the BCP system demonstrates controlled drug release contingent upon alterations in environmental pH.
Results While the use of PT2385 alone provides protective effects on chondrocytes within an inflamed environment, there exists an opportunity for further enhancement in its efficacy when administered via intra-articular injection. The BCP formulation, characterized by appropriate particle size and charge, facilitates seamless penetration into cartilage tissue. Additionally, BCP demonstrates the capability to release drugs in response to changes in environmental pH. In vitro experiments reveal that BCP effectively inhibits Hif-2α expression and catabolic factors in chondrocytes. Notably, cartilage explants and in vivo experiments indicate that BCP surpasses PT2385 alone in inhibiting the expression of HIF-2α and matrix metalloproteinase 13, particularly in the middle and deep layers.
Conclusions The BCP drug delivery system exhibits selective release of PT2385 in response to pH changes occurring during the progression of osteoarthritis (OA), thereby inhibiting HIF-2α expression deep within the cartilage. The use of BCP significantly augments the capacity of PT2385 to retard both cartilage degeneration and the progression of osteoarthritis. Consequently, BCP as an innovative approach utilizing m-CQDs to deliver PT2385 into articular cartilage, shows potential for treating osteoarthritis.This strategy opens new avenues for osteoarthritis treatment.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We are particularly grateful to all the people who have given us help on our article. Ethics approval and consent to participate. All animal experimental protocol has been carried out in accordance with relevant guidelines and all methods are reported in accordance with ARRIVE guidelines (https://arriveguidelines.org). All animal care and experimental procedures were conducted in strict adherence to the guidelines of the Experimental Animal Management Institution of Shanxi Medical University (Approval No: DW2022063). Human cartilage tissue samples were obtained from the tibial plateaus of patients who underwent knee replacement surgery at the Second Hospital of Shanxi Medical University. All patients provided written informed consent to participate in the study. The clinical study was conducted in strict adherence to the guidelines set forth by the Ethics Committee of the Second Hospital of Shanxi Medical University (Approval No. 2022.YX.No.167). Availability of data and material. All data generated or analysed during this study are included in this article. Further enquiries can be directed to the corresponding author.

Natural Science foundation of Shanxi Province, China (Grant No. 20210302123285, 202202040201012).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation and mechanism of BCP for the treatment of osteoarthritis. BCP is formulated by combining PT2385 with m-CQDs and encapsulating the complex in BSA. Upon intra-articular injection of BCP, the treatment efficiently permeates the entire cartilage layer and exhibits pH-responsive behavior during the progression of OA, allowing gradual release of PT2385. Upon entry into chondrocytes, PT2385 inhibits the binding of HIF-2α with HIF-1β, resulting in HIF-2α degradation and decreased production of downstream cartilage degradation factors, including MMPs and ADAMTS. This mechanism contributes to the protection against early cartilage degeneration.

Concentration Screening of PT2385 and Its Protective Effect on Chondrocytes in an Inflammatory Environment: a) Utilizing the real-time cell analysis (RTCA) assay, we assessed the representative cell viability of chondrocytes exposed to different concentrations of PT2385 for 72 h (n = 3). b) Western blot analysis was conducted to ascertain the representative protein expression levels of HIF-2α, HIF-1α, and HIF-1β, as well as downstream factors, following treatment with varied concentrations of PT2385 under hypoxic conditions for 48 h. c) RT-qPCR was employed to measure the relative mRNA expression levels of HIF-2α and its downstream factors in chondrocytes treated with different concentrations of PT2385 for 48 h within an IL-1β-induced inflammatory environment. Data are presented as normalized fold expression relative to the normal control group (n = 3). d) The RTCA assay was utilized to evaluate the representative cell viability of chondrocytes treated with different concentrations of PT2385 for 72 h in an IL-1β-induced inflammatory environment (n = 3). e) Western blot analysis was performed to determine the representative protein expression of HIF-2α and its downstream factors in chondrocytes treated with different concentrations of PT2385 for 48 h in an IL-1β-induced inflammatory environment. The data are presented as means ± SD. ns p > 0.05, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001. RTCA: real-time cell analysis; HIF: hypoxia-inducible factor; RT-qPCR: Real-time quantitative polymerase chain reaction; IL: interleukin.

Protective Effects of Different Concentrations of PT2385 in DMM Surgery-Induced OA: a) Representative X-ray images of mouse knee joints following intra-articular injection of varying concentrations of PT2385 for 8 weeks (n = 6). The black arrow highlights the presence of osteophytes. b) Representative fluorescence images of knee joints after intra-articular injection of Prosense probe 24 h following different concentrations of PT2385 for 8 weeks (n = 3). c) Quantification of average Region of Interest (ROI) using the small-animal fluorescence live-imaging system data (n = 3). d) Representative images of mouse knee articular tissue stained with Safranin O-Fast Green and Alcian Blue after 8 weeks of intra-articular injection of different concentrations of PT2385 (n = 6). e) Osteoarthritis Research Society International (OARSI) score for mouse cartilage using Safranin O-Fast Green staining (n = 6). Scale bar, 200 μm. The data are presented as mean ± SD. ns p > 0.05, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001. DMM: Destabilization of the Medial Meniscus; ROI: Region of Interest; OARSI: Osteoarthritis Research Society International.

Characterization of BSA-m-CQDs-PT2385: a) Schematic illustration of the preparation method for the BSA-m-CQDs-PT2385 drug delivery system. b) UV–Vis absorption spectra of PT2385, m-CQDs, CP, and BCP. c) Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of m-CQDs, CP, and BCP. d) Zeta potential of PT2385, m-CQDs, CP, and BCP. e) Hydrated particle size of PT2385, m-CQDs, CP, and BCP. f-g) Fluorescence spectra of CP and BCP. h) Comparison of the fluorescence intensity of m-CQDs, CP, and BCP excited at 450 nm. BSA: bovine serum albumin; m-CQDs: carbon quantum dots prepared using m-phenylenediamine; FTIR: Fourier-transform infrared; CP: m-CQDS-PT2385; BCP: BSA-m-CQDs-PT2385.

Detecting the pH-responsive disassembly and stability of BCP: a) Representative TEM image of BCP. Scale bar, 20 nm. b) Representative SEM images of BCP after a 12-h release in environments with pH values of 7.4 and 5.0. Scale bar, 10 nm. c) Size distribution of BCP observed under TEM. d) Hydrodynamic diameter change of BCP after a 12-h release at different pH levels (n = 3). e) Detection of dynamic release rates of CP and BCP at pH values of 7.4 and 5.0 for 120 h (n = 3). f-h) Changes in fluorescence intensity of m-CQDs, CP, and BCP at a wavelength of 450 nm after release in DMEM/F12 medium containing 10 % FBS (n = 3). BCP: BSA-m-CQDs-PT2385; TEM: transmission electron microscopy; SEM: scanning electron microscopy; CP: m-CQDS-PT2385; m-CQDs: carbon quantum dots prepared using m-phenylenediamine; DMEM/F12: Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle’s Medium: Nutrient Mixture F-12; FBS: fetal bovine serum.

The internalization of BCP by chondrocytes and its protective impact in an inflammatory environment: a) Representative images of cell imaging and cytoskeleton staining of chondrocytes loaded with BCP at 1, 6, and 24 h (n = 3). Cells were excited at 488 nm, and DAPI was used to stain cell nuclei. Scale bar, 50 μm. b) The real-time cell analysis (RTCA) assay was employed to evaluate the viability of chondrocytes subjected to IL-1β-induced inflammatory conditions after exposure to equivalent concentrations of PT2385 and BCP for 72 h (n = 3). c) Real-time quantitative polymerase chain reaction (RT-qPCR) was employed to measure the relative expression levels of HIF-2α and its downstream targets in chondrocytes exposed to IL-1β-induced inflammatory conditions after treatment with equivalent concentrations of m-CQDs, PT2385, and BCP for 48 h. The data are presented as normalized fold expression relative to the normal control group (n = 3). d) Western blot analysis was performed to assess the protein expression of HIF-2α and its downstream factors in chondrocytes exposed to the same concentration of m-CQDs, PT2385, and BCP for 48 h under IL-1β-induced inflammatory conditions. Quantified gray value ratio of target protein/β-actin (n = 3). The data are presented as means ± SD. ns p > 0.05, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001. m-CQDs: carbon quantum dots prepared using m-phenylenediamine; BCP: BSA-m-CQDs-PT2385; DAPI: 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole; RTCA: real-time cell analysis; HIF: hypoxia-inducible factor; IL: interleukin.

The distribution of BCP in cartilage tissue and its impact on inhibiting HIF-2α and MMP13: a) Protocol of cartilage explant culture and BCP loading. b) Images depicting the penetration of m-CQDs and BCP across various layers of healthy pig cartilage explants after a 12-hour loading period, visualized using a digital pathology slide scanning system on frozen tissue sections (n = 3) with excitation at 488 nm.; DAPI was used to stain cell nuclei. Boxes S, M, and D represent the surface, middle, and deep cartilage layers, respectively. Scale bar, 6× = 400 μm, 30× = 60 μm. c-d) Immunofluorescence staining was conducted to assess the expression of HIF-2α and MMP13 in various layers of early osteoarthritis (OA) human cartilage explants (n = 3) following a 72-hour loading with PT2385 and BCP. DAPI was utilized for nuclear staining. The regions labeled as S, M, and D correspond to the superficial, middle, and deep cartilage layers, respectively.Scale bar, 5× = 400 μm, 20× = 80 μm. BCP: BSA-m-CQDs-PT2385; HIF: hypoxia-inducible factor; MMP: matrix metalloproteinase; OA: osteoarthritis; m-CQDs: carbon quantum dots prepared using m-phenylenediamine; DAPI: 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole.

In vivo comparison of the protective effects of PT2385 and BCP on DMM surgery-induced early-stage OA: a) Representative X-ray images of mouse knee joints harvested after intra-articular injection of PT2385, m-CQDs, and BCP for 4 weeks (n = 6). b) Representative images of Safranin O-Fast Green and Alcian Blue staining of mouse knee joint tissue 4 weeks after intra-articular injection of PT2385, m-CQDs, and BCP (n = 6). Scale bar: 200 μm. c) Representative immunohistochemical images showing the expression of HIF-2α, MMP13, ADAMTS4, and COL-II in mouse articular cartilage 4 weeks after intra-articular injection of PT2385, m-CQDs, and BCP (n = 6). Scale bar: 100 μm. BCP: BSA-m-CQDs-PT2385; DMM: Destabilization of the Medial Meniscus; OA: osteoarthritis; m-CQDs: carbon quantum dots prepared using m-phenylenediamine; HIF: hypoxia-inducible factor; MMP: matrix metalloproteinase; ADAMTS: a disintegrin and metalloproteinase with thrombospondin motifs; COL-II: collagen type II.

head.ce:title: A pH-responsive novel delivery system utilizing carbon quantum dots loaded with PT2385 for targeted inhibition of HIF-2α in the treatment of osteoarthritis

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Osteoarthritis (OA) is a progressive joint disorder marked by the degradation of cartilage. Elevated concentrations of hypoxia-inducible factor-2α (HIF-2α) are intricately linked to the pathological development of OA. PT2385 has demonstrated effective inhibition of HIF-2α, thereby potentially impeding the initial advancement of OA. Nevertheless, challenges persist, including limited penetration into the deeper layers of cartilage, issues related to charge rejection, and a heightened rate of clearance from the joint. These constraints necessitate further consideration and exploration.

It has been demonstrated that PT2385 exhibits efficient inhibition of HIF-2α expression, thereby contributing to the delay in the progression of osteoarthritis. The pH-responsive attributes of carbon quantum dots, specifically those employing m-phenylenediamine (m-CQDs) coated with bovine serum albumin (BSA), have been systematically evaluated. In both in vitro settings involving cartilage explants and in vivo experiments, the efficacy of BSA-m-CQDs-PT2385 (BCP) has been confirmed in facilitating the transport of PT2385 to the middle and deep layers of cartilage. Furthermore, the BCP system demonstrates controlled drug release contingent upon alterations in environmental pH.

While the use of PT2385 alone provides protective effects on chondrocytes within an inflamed environment, there exists an opportunity for further enhancement in its efficacy when administered via intra-articular injection. The BCP formulation, characterized by appropriate particle size and charge, facilitates seamless penetration into cartilage tissue. Additionally, BCP demonstrates the capability to release drugs in response to changes in environmental pH. In vitro experiments reveal that BCP effectively inhibits Hif-2α expression and catabolic factors in chondrocytes. Notably, cartilage explants and in vivo experiments indicate that BCP surpasses PT2385 alone in inhibiting the expression of HIF-2α and matrix metalloproteinase 13, particularly in the middle and deep layers.

The BCP drug delivery system exhibits selective release of PT2385 in response to pH changes occurring during the progression of osteoarthritis (OA), thereby inhibiting HIF-2α expression deep within the cartilage. The use of BCP significantly augments the capacity of PT2385 to retard both cartilage degeneration and the progression of osteoarthritis. Consequently, BCP as an innovative approach utilizing m-CQDs to deliver PT2385 into articular cartilage, shows potential for treating osteoarthritis.This strategy opens new avenues for osteoarthritis treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Osteoarthritis (OA) is a chronic articular condition associated with pain and disability, particularly prevalent in the elderly population (Glyn-Jones et al., 2015, Hiligsmann et al., 2013, Frank et al., 2019). The disease impacts all articular tissues and is characterized by the progressive degeneration of articular cartilage, vascular invasion of the articular surface, subchondral osteosclerosis, bone remodeling in the marrow, blood vessel formation, and synovitis at the osteochondral interface (Glyn-Jones et al., 2015, Loeser et al., 2012, Weinans et al., 2012, Scanzello and Goldring, 2012). Despite advancements in comprehending the pathogenesis of OA, effective early prevention and treatment options remain limited in clinical practice (Katz et al., 2021). Consequently, researchers have directed their efforts toward developing innovative approaches for the early restoration of damaged cartilage tissues. Notably, it is imperative to acknowledge the absence of regulatory-approved disease-modifying OA drugs for clinical use, attributed to the distinctive environment of the articular cavity and cartilage (Martel-Pelletier et al., 2016). Given these challenges, there is a pressing need for drugs capable of repairing cartilage in clinical applications to impede the progression of OA.

Studies on the molecular mechanisms underlying cartilage degeneration have identified several regulatory genes responsible for maintaining the delicate balance between cartilage catabolic and anabolic metabolism. Various catabolic factors, including matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs) and a disintegrin and metalloproteinase with thrombospondin motifs (ADAMTS), play a key role in inducing cartilage degeneration (Hochberg et al., 2013; Motta et al., 2023; Boer et al., 2021). The regulation of these factors is orchestrated by hypoxia-inducible factor-2α (HIF-2α) (Murphy, 2010, Yang et al., 2010, Lee et al., 2016), rendering it a significant mediator and a potential therapeutic target for osteoarthritis (OA) (Lee et al., 2016).

Functioning as a critical transcription factor for cellular adaptation to hypoxia in cartilage and the progression of inflammation, HIF-2α specifically binds to hypoxia response elements and translocates into the nucleus. This process enhances the promoter activity of key genes such as collagen type X (COL-X), matrix metalloproteinase 13 (MMP13), and vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) (Wu et al., 2015, Saito et al., 2010). HIF-2α exerts influence over the growth, hypertrophic differentiation, and extracellular matrix (ECM) synthesis of chondrocytes, thereby impacting the stability and function of articular cartilage (Saito et al., 2010). Although HIF-2α presents itself as a promising target for delaying early articular cartilage degeneration, agents with effective inhibitory properties against HIF-2α have not been comprehensively explored.

PT2385, an oral small-molecule drug designed to target HIF-2α, has been developed and demonstrates efficacy by inhibiting the binding of HIF-2α and HIF-1β, consequently leading to the degradation of HIF-2α (Wehn et al., 2018, Chen et al., 2016). While PT2385 is primarily utilized in the treatment of renal cell carcinoma (RCC) (Chen et al., 2016, Martínez-Sáez et al., 2017), its application in osteoarthritis (OA) treatment has not been explored. Given its pharmacological effects, there is a plausible hypothesis that PT2385 may serve as an effective targeted drug for OA. However, the delivery of PT2385 to the articular cartilage presents significant challenges. The foremost challenge is the elevated clearance rate within the articular cavity. The synovial fluid, characterized by high viscosity, complex biological composition, and efficient clearance capability, compromises the stability of the drug and impedes its diffusion (Pradal et al., 2016, Champion et al., 2008). Secondly, the dense network and negative charge inherent in the articular cartilage extracellular matrix (ECM) pose obstacles to PT2385, which carries a positive charge, in penetrating the deep layers of the cartilage to exert its therapeutic effects (Ng et al., 2003, Gerwin et al., 2006). Given that HIF-2α is expressed in the middle and deep layers of the cartilage, the penetration of PT2385 throughout the entire cartilage layer is essential for reaching chondrocytes and the ECM target (Evans, 2016). Consequently, it becomes imperative to design drug carrier systems with optimal size and charge characteristics to facilitate penetration throughout the entire cartilage and ensure the sustained retention of PT2385.

In recent years, nanoscale drug delivery systems have gained substantial attention due to rapid advancements in nanotechnology, offering the potential to enhance therapeutic efficiency while minimizing side effects (Kang and Im, 2014, Gambaro et al., 2021). Nanoparticles, therefore, hold considerable promise for arthritis treatment by efficiently penetrating the entire cartilage layer (Zhang et al., 2022). Previous successful studies have synthesized carbon quantum dots using m-phenylenediamine (m-CQDs) (Zhang et al., 2021). These m-CQDs, emitting green fluorescence through the reaction between m-phenylenediamine and L-cysteine, have demonstrated the ability permeate the cartilage surface in cartilage tissue and demonstrate favorable biosafety (Guo et al., 2022). Given the increasing acidity of the cartilage and synovial membrane during the progression of osteoarthritis (Cummings and Nordby, 1966, Jebens and Monk-Jones, 1959, Lombardi et al., 2022), the pH-responsive nature of carbon quantum dots, combined with drugs through electrostatic binding and bovine serum albumin (BSA) packaging, can be further harnessed (Duan et al., 2022). Consequently, m-CQDs possess the potential to serve as exceptional carriers for PT2385, functioning as a multifunctional drug delivery system.

In this study, we proposed a methodology for fabricating m-CQDS-PT2385 (CP) by combining PT2385 with m-CQDs through electrostatic bonding. BSA-m-CQDS-PT2385 (BCP) was then generated by encapsulating bovine serum albumin (BSA) as a protective shell. The positive charge of the m-CQDs enables them to bind to PT2385, which carries a negative surface charge, through electrostatic adsorption. In an acidic environment, the electrostatic adsorption weakens, leading to the gradual release of PT2385. This drug-loading system exhibits pH-responsive characteristics (Hawkins et al., 2008, Ehtesabi et al., 2020). BSA serves as a protein shell, creating novel lipoprotein nanocarriers and thereby contributing to the stabilization of the system (Wan et al., 2015, Kahya and Erim, 2019). This design safeguards CP from the impact of environmental changes, preventing rapid separation and ensuring an extended drug-release duration. In acidic conditions, the hydrophobic bond between BSA molecules and the CP drug-carrying system is disrupted, thereby diminishing the electrostatic adsorption between m-CQDs and PT2385 and augmenting the pH-responsive capability of the system. Upon intra-articular injection of BCP, as the articular cartilage undergoes degeneration, both the articular cavity and the entire cartilage region experience acidification. This swift acidification prompts the opening of the BSA shell, exposing CP and facilitating the release of PT2385. Consequently, this treatment targets the elimination of HIF-2α, a byproduct of articular cartilage degeneration, and mitigates the production of various downstream cartilage matrix degradation factors, including MMP3, MMP13, ADAMTS4, and COL-X. Our research introduces a novel treatment for osteoarthritis by applying PT2385 and using m-CQDs to create a drug delivery system for intra-articular injection. This approach effectively overcomes previous challenges with small molecule drugs in cartilage treatment. Our systematic evaluation shows it significantly delays cartilage degeneration and removes HIF-2α, offering a new clinical solution with significant implications.
Materials and apparatus.

PT2385 was procured from MCE Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). m-Phenylenediamine and L-cysteine were acquired from Aladdin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Bovine serum albumin (BSA) and bicinchoninic acid (BCA) protein assay kits were sourced from Beyotime Co., Ltd. (Wuhan, China). Primers for quantitative real-time polymerase chain reaction (qRT-PCR) (Table S1) were obtained from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). PrimeScript™ RT Master Mix and TB Green™ Premix Ex Taq™ II were purchased from TAKARA Bio Co., Ltd. (Kusatsu, Japan). Antibodies for HIF-1α, HIF-2α, HIF-1β, SOX9, GLUT-1, VEGF, ADAMTS4, MMP13, MMP3, COL-II, and β-ACTIN were sourced from Abcam (Cambridge, UK). IL-1β recombinant protein was acquired from Peprotech Co., Ltd. (Rocky Hill, NJ, USA). The probe was obtained from Perkin Elmer (Waltham, MA, USA). Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E), Alcian Blue, and cell skeleton staining kits, along with dialysis bags, were procured from Solarbio Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China), cytoskeleton staining was performed using rhodamine-labeled phalloidin to label the F-actin in cells. Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle’s Medium: Nutrient Mixture F-12 (DMEM/F12) culture medium, fetal bovine serum (FBS), and TRIzol reagent were sourced from Thermo Fisher Scientific Co., Ltd. (Waltham, MA, USA).

ATDC5 cells were procured from the Cell Bank of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China) and were cultured in Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle’s Medium: Nutrient Mixture F-12 (DMEM/F12). The complete culture medium was prepared by supplementing DMEM/F12 with 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS), 100 U·ml-1 penicillin, and 0.1 mg·ml-1 streptomycin (Solarbio Co., Ltd.). ATDC5 cells were maintained at 37 °C in a humidified incubator with 5 % CO2. All cells utilized in the experiments were in the logarithmic growth phase.

Hypoxic culture of cells: Anaerocult boxes and anaerocult sachets were purchased from Mitsubishi MGC Co., Ltd. (Tokyo, Japan). Oxygen concentration in the Anaerocult Box was maintained at 5 %. After loading PT2385 at concentrations of 2.5, 5, 10, and 20 μmol·ml-1, cells were placed in the Anaerocult Box for 48 h before subsequent experiments.

Construction of the culture environment for inflammatory chondrocytes: Chondrocytes were seeded in six-well plate at 80–90 % confluence. After the cells entered the logarithmic growth period, the medium was refreshed and drug intervention was initiated. After 12 h, IL-β recombinant protein with a final concentration of 20 μmol·ml-1 was added to the medium.Cells were collected for follow-up experiments after a 48-h intervention period.

The eight-week-old male C57BL/6 mice were acquired from the Experimental Animal Center of Shanxi Medical University. Pig cartilage tissue samples were sourced from the articular cartilage of the tibial plateau in the knee joints of 18-month-old adult pigs. All aspects of animal care and experimental procedures were meticulously carried out in strict accordance with the guidelines established by the Experimental Animal Management Institution of Shanxi Medical University (Approval No: DW2022063).

Human cartilage tissue samples were collected from the tibial plateaus of individuals who underwent knee replacement surgery at the Second Hospital of Shanxi Medical University. The samples were obtained from patients diagnosed with “early cartilage degeneration.” All participants provided written informed consent to partake in the study. The research procedures were conducted in strict adherence to the guidelines established by the Ethics Committee of the Second Hospital of Shanxi Medical University (Approval No. 2022.YX.No.167).

Real-time cell analysis (RTCA) was conducted utilizing the RTCA S16 system (ACEA Biosciences Co., Ltd., San Diego, CA, USA). Real-time quantitative polymerase chain reaction (RT-qPCR) results were obtained using the QuantStudio™ 6 Flex Real-Time PCR system (Thermo Fisher Scientific Co., Ltd.). Visualization and quantification of Western blot results were performed using a gel imaging system (Bio-Rad Laboratories Co., Ltd., Hercules, CA, USA). Mouse knee articular radiographs were acquired using a small-animal X-ray system (Faxitron Bioptics LLC, Tucson, AZ, USA). The ProSense metabolic factor fluorescence signal in mouse cartilage tissue was monitored using a small-animal fluorescence live-imaging system (Caliper IVIS Lumina, Perkin Elmer Co., Ltd., Waltham, MA, USA). Slice images were obtained using a digital pathology slide scanner (Pannoramic MIDI, 3DHISTECH Co., Ltd., Budapest, Hungary).

m-CQDs were synthesized in a stainless-steel box-type resistance furnace (Boxun Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China). UV–visible absorption spectra were recorded during the drug-loading process using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi Co., Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). Infrared absorption spectra were obtained using a Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer (VARIAN Co., Ltd., Palo Alto, CA, USA), while fluorescence spectra and fluorescence intensity measurements were conducted using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi Co., Ltd.). The hydrated particle size and zeta potential of the materials were determined employing a dynamic light-scattering particle size analyzer (MALVERN Instruments Limited, Malvern, UK). The morphological characteristics and particle size of the drug delivery system were assessed using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Thermo Fisher Scientific Co., Ltd.). Morphological changes in the drug delivery system were observed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Thermo Fisher Scientific Co., Ltd.).
Destabilization of the medial meniscus (DMM) surgery-induced OA in a mouse model.

To simulate human chronic osteoarthritis (OA), destabilization of the medial meniscotibial ligament (DMM) surgery was performed on male C57BL/6 mice (8 weeks old, n = 24). Pentobarbital sodium inhalation anesthesia was administered before the surgical procedure. The right knee joint of the mice was surgically opened, and the medial meniscotibial ligament (MMTL) was severed to induce joint destabilization. After confirming the rupture of the medial meniscal ligament, the joint and surgical incision were meticulously sutured layer by layer. In the sham group (n = 6), the knee joint was exposed without severing the MMTL, and the joints were closed with sutures. Following the surgery, the mice were allowed unrestricted movement and provided with ample food and water. The knee joint wounds of the mice were closely monitored for proper healing for 2 weeks postoperatively before further treatment was initiated. In assessing the effectiveness of different concentrations of PT2385 on the progression of osteoarthritis, we used the 8-week time point post-DMM surgery in mice to evaluate the drug’s impact on cartilage degeneration.
Intra-articular injection of varied PT2385 doses and assessment of OA severity in mouse knee.

Male C57BL/6 mice (8 weeks old, n = 24), subjected to destabilization of the medial meniscus (DMM), were randomly allocated into four groups. These groups received intra-articular injections of PT2385 at doses of 0.01, 0.1, and 1 mg·ml-1·kg-1 every 3 days in the right knee articular cavity. The sham group and vehicle group were administered equivalent volumes of saline injections as controls. Eight weeks post-surgery, a ProSense probe (10 μL) was injected into the knee articular cavity, and the small-animal fluorescence live-imaging system was employed to evaluate the expression levels of catabolic factors in the knee articular cavity. Subsequently, the mice were euthanized, and the right knee joints were examined using a small-animal X-ray system. The knee joints were sectioned continuously at a thickness of 5 μm and stained with Safranin O-Fast Green and Alcian Blue.
Synthesis of m-CQDs.

To synthesize the m-CQDs, 800 mg of L-cysteine and 500 mg of m-phenylenediamine were combined in 30 mL of ultrapure water. The mixture was then transferred to a reaction vessel lined with Teflon and subjected to heating at 180 °C for 8 h in a stainless-steel box-resistance furnace. Following the reaction, impurities were removed from the mixture using a 5 mm filter. Any remaining minor impurities were further eliminated through dialysis using a dialysis bag with a cutoff of 3500 Da. Ultimately, the solution was vacuum freeze-dried to yield pure m-CQDs.
Binding of m-CQDs with PT2385.

The pure m-CQDs were dissolved in ultrapure water. Subsequently, the m-CQD solution was combined with varying concentrations of the PT2385 solution. The resulting mixture was stirred using a magnetic stirrer in a dark environment at a temperature of 37 °C for 24 h. Following this, the mixture was transferred into a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff of 1000 Da to eliminate any excess PT2385. The mass of free PT2385 (A0) was measured utilizing the UV absorption method. Subsequently, the mass of PT2385 bound to m-CQDs was calculated based on the total mass of PT2385 (A1). The drug-loading efficiency(Fig. S1a) formula is as follows:
Drug-loading efficiency (wt %) = (A1 − A0) / A1 × 100 %.
The resulting solution underwent vacuum freeze-drying to yield pure CP powder.
Coating CP with BSA.

CP was dissolved in ultrapure water, and the resulting CP solution was combined with the BSA solution at various concentration ratios in equal volumes. The BCP drug delivery system was formed through magnetic stirring. The residual BSA and BCP in the solution were then precipitated using the salt precipitation method, followed by freeze-drying to produce a coated BCP drug delivery system. The supernatant was collected from the salt precipitation solution, and the mass of free CP (B0) was determined using the UV absorption method. The coating efficiency of BSA. (Fig. S1b) was calculated as (B1 − B0) / B1 × 100 %, where B1 represents the total mass of CP added.
Characterization of BCP.

The preparation and mechanism of BCP for the treatment of osteoarthritis are depicted in Fig. 1 . Solutions of m-CQDs, PT2385, CP, and BCP were prepared, and their physicochemical properties were characterized. Ultraviolet (UV) spectra were recorded using a UV–visible spectrophotometer. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was employed to obtain the infrared spectra. Additionally, the size and zeta potential of the hydrated particles were assessed using a dynamic light-scattering particle size analyzer. Fluorescence spectra were measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer, with fluorescence intensity recorded at 450 nm.
pH-responsiveness and stability testing of BCP.

The CP and BCP solutions were prepared in ultrapure water, and the pH of the solutions was adjusted to 7.4, 6.6, 5.8, and 5.0 using hydrochloric acid. BCP was allowed to release gradually in the solution for 12 h, and the morphology of BCP was subsequently observed at pH 7.4 and 5.0 using scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Additionally, we analyzed the hydrodynamic diameters of CP and BCP in different pH environments using a dynamic light-scattering particle size analyzer. The solutions containing CP and BCP were individually exposed to environments with pH values of 7.4 and 5.0, respectively, for sustained release over a 72-hour period. A dialysis bag was employed to separate and quantify the mass of free PT2385 (C0) in the sustained-release system. The cumulative release rate of PT2385 was calculated as follows: C0/C1 × 100 %, where C1 represents the total mass of PT2385 added. In fluorescence stability testing, equal masses of m-CQDs, CP, and BCP were introduced into DMEM/F12 medium with 10 % FBS, and the release was permitted for 50 min. Alterations in fluorescence intensity were recorded at 450 nm at 10-minute intervals.
Absorption rate of BCP in chondrocytes.

The CP and BCP solutions were prepared in ultrapure water, add it to the adherent chondrocytes at a concentration of 10 µg/ml. Use plain culture medium as a blank control to measure the initial fluorescence intensity of BCP (C1). Collect culture medium samples at 1 h, 6 h, and 24 h, and measure the fluorescence intensity of free BCP at 450 nm (C0). Calculate the BCP absorption efficiency in chondrocytes. The absorption efficiency formula is as follows:
Absorption efficiency = (C1 − C0) / C1 × 100 %.
Cartilage explant culture.

Following the removal of the overlying muscle, the tibial plateau was sectioned into cylindrical cartilage explants measuring 5 mm in diameter and 6 mm in height. Modified acrylic acid ester was utilized to seal all regions of the cartilage explants, ensuring exclusive access to the surface of the cartilage. This sealing process guaranteed that PT2385, m-CQDs, and BCP gradually penetrated from the surface layer to the deeper layers of the cartilage. The sealed cartilage explants were then cultured in a complete culture medium. Throughout the experiment, PT2385, m-CQDs, and BCP at a dose of 1 mg·ml-1 were individually introduced into the complete culture medium. The efficacy of different drug delivery systems in penetrating cartilage tissue and inhibiting deep-layer HIF-2α in the cartilage was subsequently assessed. Following a 12-hour culture of healthy pig cartilage explants, the modified acrylic acid ester was removed, and the cartilage explants were retrieved. A cryostat microtome was employed to generate 10 μm sections for assessing BCP penetration throughout the entire cartilage layer. After culturing early OA human cartilage explants for 72 h, the cartilage explants were retrieved using the aforementioned method. Subsequently, 5 μm sections were produced using a paraffin microtome for immunofluorescent staining of HIF-2α and MMP13 across the complete cartilage layer. The immunofluorescent staining results were quantified using ImageJ software (version 1.53 k).
Metabolism of BCP in the mouse joint.

To determine the injection cycle of BCP in intra-articular administration, we conducted right intra-articular injections of BCP at a dose of 0.1 mg·ml-1·kg-1 in 8-week-old male C57BL/6 mice. The normal control group (n = 3) received equivalent volumes of saline injections. At specific time points post-injection, which were 12 h (n = 3), 1 day (n = 3), 3 days (n = 3), 5 days (n = 3), and 7 days (n = 3), the mice were euthanized. Following decalcification and dehydration, the joints were sectioned into 10 μm slices using a cryostat microtome. The resulting slices were examined to observe the distribution of fluorescence in the articular tissue.
Metabolic rate, biosafety, and effectiveness of intra-articular injection of BCP.

To assess the impact of BCP intra-articular injection on the progression of early OA, we expedited the sampling timeline after DMM surgery. DMM models were established in 8-week-old male C57BL/6 mice (n = 24), with six mice in the sham group that did not undergo MMTL severing. The 24 mice subjected to DMM surgery were randomly allocated into four groups: PT2385 group (n = 6), receiving intra-articular injections of 1 mg·ml-1·kg-1 PT2385 every 3 days; m-CQD group (n = 6); and BCP group (n = 6), receiving intra-articular injections of 0.1 mg·ml-1·kg-1 once a week. The vehicle group received an equivalent volume of saline. After 6 weeks post-surgery, the mice were euthanized, and vital organs (heart, liver, and kidney) were collected, sectioned (5 μm), and stained with H&E. Eyelid venous blood was collected under anticoagulation conditions at the conclusion of the treatment. Liver and kidney function, along with blood biochemical parameters, were assessed using a serum detection system. Sections (5 μm) were prepared and stained using Safranin O-Fast Green and Alcian Blue. Immunohistochemical staining of HIF-2α, ADAMTS4, MMP13, and COL-II was conducted on the cartilage from each group of mice. Quantification of immunohistochemical staining results was carried out using ImageJ software (version 1.53 k).
Statistical analysis.

All experiments were conducted in triplicate. The data are presented as the mean ± standard error of the mean and were statistically analyzed using a two-sided Student’s t-test. For significance testing of more than two sample groups, we employed one-way ANOVA and conducted pairwise comparisons using the LSD method. Descriptive statistics were used to analyze the UV spectra, infrared spectra, fluorescence spectra, Zeta potential, particle size distribution from TEM, and hydrodynamic diameter of m-CQDs, CP, and BCP. A significance level of p < 0.05 was considered statistically significant. The statistical analyses were carried out using SPSS software for Windows (version 24.0; SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA).
Effectiveness of PT2385 in chondrocytes.

A preliminary assessment of pharmacological effects is imperative as PT2385 is being introduced for the first time in the context of osteoarthritis (OA). Chondrocytes were cultured under hypoxic conditions, and the RTCA assay was employed to ascertain a safe concentration of PT2385. It was observed that PT2385 inhibited chondrocyte proliferation at concentrations exceeding 20 μM. (Fig. 2 a, Fig. S2a); Consequently, the safe concentration of PT2385 for chondrocytes was established at 20 μM. At this concentration, we examined the eradication impact of PT2385 on HIF-2α and its subsequent factors in chondrocytes. HIF-2α was efficiently eliminated at a concentration of 2.5 μM, while downstream factors, including VEGF and GLUT-1, exhibited significant inhibition at concentrations ranging from 10 to 20 μM. (Fig. 2b, Fig. S2b). Hence, the optimal drug concentration of PT2385 for chondrocytes was determined to be in the range of 10–20 μM. Chondrocytes exposed to IL-1β stimulation, simulating the inflammatory environment of OA, exhibited effective suppression of HIF-2α overexpression when treated with PT2385 at concentrations of 10–20 μM (Fig. 2e). Moreover, the expression levels of Hif-1α and Hif-1β in chondrocytes remained unaltered (Fig. S3c-d). Given the intricate role of HIF-1α in chondrocytes (Husa et al., 2010, Zhang et al., 2015), the specific targeting of HIF-2α by PT2385 indicates more favorable clinical prospects. PT2385 demonstrated effective inhibition of downstream proteins implicated in cartilage matrix degradation and chondrocyte hypertrophy, including MMPs, Col-X, and runt-related transcription factor 2 (Runx2) (Fig. 2e). We evaluated the proliferation rate of chondrocytes treated with PT2385 under inflammatory conditions. PT2385 at a concentration of 20 μm increased chondrocyte activity, resulting in the restoration of the cell proliferation rate. (Fig. 2d, Fig. S3b). Protein detection of proliferating cell nuclear antigen (PCNA) corroborated these findings. (Fig. 2e). These findings suggest that PT2385 efficiently suppresses HIF-2α and its downstream factors, providing effective protection to chondrocytes in an inflammatory environment.
Effectiveness of PT2385 on DMM surgery-induced OA.

To assess the effectiveness and optimal concentration of intra-articular PT2385 injection in osteoarthritis (OA), we administered PT2385 at doses of 0.01, 0.1, and 1 mg·ml-1·kg-1 to mice that had undergone destabilization of the medial meniscus (DMM) surgery. X-ray images of the mouse knee joints revealed pronounced osteophyte formation, increased subchondral bone density, and calcification of the meniscus and ligaments in the vehicle group and the 0.01 mg·ml-1·kg-1 PT2385 injection group compared to the sham group. In contrast, intra-articular injection of PT2385 at 0.1 and 1 mg·ml-1·kg-1 effectively reduced osteophyte formation in the mouse knee joints, with the meniscus and ligaments displaying changes more akin to those in the sham group mice (Fig. 3 a). We assessed the production of matrix degradation factors in the knee joints of mice using ProSense probes. The production gradually decreased with increasing intra-articular drug concentrations, and the most favorable effect was observed at 1 mg·ml-1·kg-1 (Fig. 3b-c). Safranin O and Alcian Blue staining of mouse knee joints revealed the status of cartilage degradation and extracellular matrix (ECM) loss. The articular cartilage surfaces of mice in the sham group appeared intact, ordered, and smooth, with a uniform distribution of glycosaminoglycans (GAG) in the ECM, as evidenced by strong Safranin O staining (Fig. 3d). In contrast, the cartilage layer in the vehicle group and the 0.01 mg·ml-1·kg-1 PT2385 injection group exhibited significant loss, with evident vertical cracks and erosions. Mice in the 0.1 and 1 mg·ml-1·kg-1 PT2385 injection groups displayed reduced cartilage matrix loss and less damage. Notably, the 1 mg·ml-1·kg-1 PT2385 injection group exhibited a relatively intact cartilage surface, strong Safranin O staining, and a cartilage thickness closer to that of the sham group compared to the surgery group. Osteoarthritis Research Society International (OARSI) scores revealed no statistically significant difference between the 0.01 mg·ml-1·kg-1 PT2385 injection group and the vehicle group. However, these groups exhibited statistically significant differences compared to the 0.1 and 1 mg·ml-1·kg-1 PT2385 injection groups. Furthermore, among the different PT2385 injection groups, the 1 mg·ml-1·kg-1 PT2385 injection group displayed the lowest OARSI scores (Fig. 3e). These findings suggest that the intra-articular injection of high-concentration PT2385 provides protective benefits to the articular cartilage in the early stages of OA. PT2385 effectively promotes the deposition of glycosaminoglycans (GAG), helps maintain cartilage thickness, and slows extracellular matrix (ECM) degradation. Consequently, PT2385 shows potential as a treatment for early-stage OA.
Characterization of BCP.

Due to the negative charge characteristics of PT2385, it experiences repulsion from cartilage tissues with highly negative charges. Furthermore, its low molecular weight leads to the swift clearance of PT2385 from the articular cavity. This requires an escalation in the number of intra-articular injections and injection concentrations in animal experiments, which constrains the clinical application of PT2385. Consequently, we devised a BCP system to facilitate the efficient delivery of PT2385 into the articular cavity (Fig. 4 a). The analysis of the binding rate between PT2385 and m-CQDs shows that the maximum binding ratio is achieved at a 55:1 ratio of PT2385 to m-CQDs (Fig. S1a). Additionally, the coating rate analysis indicates that BSA and CP reach the maximum coating ratio at a 1.4:1 ratio, with a maximum coating rate of approximately 70 % (Fig. S1b).The results indicate that each m-CQDs nanoparticle can load a substantial amount of PT2385 drug, which not only enhances the drug loading capacity but also significantly improves the drug delivery efficiency of the system in tissues. A higher drug loading amount contributes to increasing the therapeutic dose, thereby enhancing the efficacy. Additionally, the higher BSA coating rate further ensures the stability of the drug delivery system. Under these conditions,we assessed the physicochemical properties of CP and BCP. The UV absorption spectra indicated strong absorption in both the UV and visible light regions for these systems. The UV absorption peaks of CP and BCP closely mirrored those of the m-CQDs. However, CP and BCP showed slightly weaker UV absorption peaks at 403 nm compared to the m-CQDs. This observation is likely attributed to the reduction in absorption peak intensity resulting from the loading of PT2385 and the coating with BSA (Fig. 4b).

We conducted FTIR spectroscopy to characterize the surface functional groups of the drug delivery system (Fig. 4c). The FTIR spectra of the pure m-CQDs were consistent with those of previously synthesized m-CQDs. The peak at 3448 cm-1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of the N–H and O–H bonds, while the peak at 1790 cm-1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of C=O bonds. Peaks at 2927, 1730, 1496, 1294, and 1164 cm-1 could be attributed to the stretching vibrations of the C–H, C=C, C-N, C-O, and C-S bonds, respectively, indicating the successful synthesis of the m-CQDs (Qu et al., 2013, Xu et al., 2014, Arcudi et al., 2016). In the m-CQDs-PT2385 system, a new peak emerged at 1120 cm-1, attributed to the stretching vibration of the loaded O=S=O bond on PT2385 (Wehn et al., 2018). This indicated the successful loading of PT2385 onto the m-CQDs. The new peak at 1120 cm-1 disappeared in the BCP system, and a new peak corresponding to the stretching vibration of C-OH appeared at 1050 cm-1, indicating that BSA completely encapsulated m-CQDs-PT2385, forming the BCP system.

Subsequent to an in-depth examination of the drug delivery system, our emphasis shifted toward scrutinizing the hydrodynamic dimensions and the prospect of formulating a drug carrier system tailored for cartilage tissue. Cartilage tissue, characterized by a pronounced negative charge, possesses a limited effective pore size of merely 6 nm. This restriction, attributed to the molecular sieving effect, implies that entities either exceeding or falling short of optimal size parameters may persist on the cartilage surface, precluding infiltration into its underlying layers (Leddy et al., 2003, DiDomenico et al., 2017). Hence, the design of a suitable drug carrier system is imperative for the efficacious treatment of osteoarthritis (OA). Due to its negative charge and diminutive particle size, PT2385 encounters challenges in penetrating cartilage tissue, leading to rapid clearance. To overcome this hurdle, we devised a strategy involving the amalgamation of m-CQDs (positively charged) with PT2385, enveloped within a BSA protective shell. This synergistic approach, involving positively charged m-CQDs and BSA encapsulation, yielded a positively charged drug delivery system characterized by a hydrated particle size of approximately 68 nm (Fig. 4d-e). This configuration aligns with the specifications of an ideal drug carrier system tailored for cartilage tissue (DiDomenico et al., 2018), guaranteeing unimpeded penetration into the articular cartilage despite its negative charge. Moreover, the judicious particle size enables enhanced infiltration into the cartilage, facilitating the controlled release of the drug to effectively inhibit HIF-2α.

Given the inherent green fluorescence of m-CQDs (Zhang et al., 2021), it is reasonable to expect that the drug delivery system inherits fluorescent properties. This characteristic proves advantageous in tracing the distribution of the drug carrier system within the cartilage tissue. Utilizing a fluorescence spectrometer, we analyzed the fluorescence spectra of the system during synthesis. The fluorescence spectra of CP and BCP closely resembled those of m-CQDs (Zhang et al., 2021), showcasing excitation wavelength dependence and a peak fluorescence intensity at 450 nm (Fig. 4f-g, Fig. S1e). This observation suggests that the drug carrier system indeed exhibits green fluorescence. Nevertheless, it's worth noting that the fluorescence intensity of CP at 450 nm was lower than that of the m-CQDs, and BCP displayed an even lower intensity. This phenomenon can be attributed to the loading of PT2385 onto the m-CQDs and the subsequent coating with BSA(Fig. 4h). These outcomes validate the successful creation of the BCP drug delivery system. Much like m-CQDs, this system displayed green fluorescence. The positive charge and hydrodynamic size characteristics suggest that the system is not swiftly eliminated by cartilage tissue, signifying its ability to persist within the cartilage and release drugs in a sustained manner.
pH-responsive disassembly and stability of BCP.

Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) was utilized to investigate the morphology and particle size of the BCP drug delivery system. The TEM images demonstrate a homogeneous distribution of BCP particles (Fig. 5 a), following a normal distribution pattern, with an average size of approximately 6 nm (Fig. 5c). The porous nature of cartilage tissue facilitates the deep penetration and effective diffusion of nanoparticles into its inner layers (Leddy et al., 2003, Bajpayee et al., 2014). In comparison with small-molecule drugs or larger particles, these nanosized particles exhibit superior penetration and diffusion properties within the cartilage tissue.The interplay of hydrophobic interactions between BSA and m-CQDs, along with the electrostatic binding between m-CQDs and PT2385, imparts high sensitivity to environmental pH in the system. This sensitivity leads to facile dissociation under acidic conditions (Ehtesabi et al., 2020, Huang et al., 2018), showcasing a pronounced pH-responsive behavior. To explore the pH-responsive dissociation of the BCP drug delivery system, alterations in hydrodynamic size, morphology, and the PT2385 drug-release profile were assessed across distinct pH conditions. SEM images revealed that near a pH of 7.4, BSA effectively coated the m-CQDs-PT2385 particles, resulting in a uniformly spherical and stable system. With a decrease in pH to 5.0, the BSA shell underwent decomposition, resulting in the dissociation of m-CQDs-PT2385 and the exposure of m-CQDs (Fig. 5b). As the acidity intensified, the hydrated particle size of the drug carrier system gradually increased, especially at pH 5.8 and 5.0, resembling conditions at the mid-to-late stage of osteoarthritis (OA) (Lombardi et al., 2022). The hydrated particle sizes of the nanoparticles significantly increased to approximately 164 nm and 220 nm, respectively (Fig. 5d), indicating that the drug carrier system became more porous, thereby facilitating drug release.

We also assessed the drug-release profiles of BCP and CP at different pH values (Fig. 5e). The drug release behavior of both CP and BCP systems adheres to a first-order kinetic model. The drug release rate constants were calculated to evaluate the release rates of the different systems. At a pH of 7.4, the release rate constants for BCP and CP were 52.19 and 68.84, respectively, with BCP demonstrating a lower release rate constant than CP, indicating a slower drug release rate. However, when the pH decreased to 5.0, the release rate constants for BCP and CP were 89.68 and 82.23, respectively, with BCP showing a higher release rate constant than CP, signifying a faster drug release rate. This indicates that BCP has a more pronounced pH-responsive drug release capability, facilitating a quicker release of PT2385 in acidic environments. Additionally, we evaluated the fluorescence stability of the system. The m-CQDs demonstrated robust fluorescence stability in DMEM/F12 culture medium supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS), maintaining consistent fluorescence intensity for up to 50 min. However, m-CQDs-PT2385 exhibited a decrease in fluorescence intensity within the same timeframe. Following BSA coating, the reduction in the fluorescence intensity of the drug carrier system appeared mitigated (Fig. 5f-h), suggesting that the BSA coating enhanced its stability. These findings highlight that BCP exhibits pH-responsive degradation, allowing for selective release in accordance with the progression of osteoarthritis (OA).
Effectiveness of BCP in chondrocytes.

To assess the cytotoxicity of BCP and identify its optimal concentration for use in chondrocytes, BCP was applied to chondrocytes at different concentrations, ranging from 10 to 1000 μg·ml-1, for a duration of 120 h. Within the concentration range of 10 to 100 μg·ml-1, the drug carrier system exhibited a negative impact on cell proliferation. This implies that 10 μg·ml-1 is the safe concentration of BCP in the extracellular culture system of chondrocytes (Fig. S4a-b).

Subsequently, we explored the capability of BCP to permeate chondrocytes and release PT2385 by subjecting chondrocytes to varying BCP concentrations ranging from 0 to 10 μg·ml-1 for 72 h. Green fluorescence became discernible inside the cells when the drug carrier system reached a concentration of 1 μg·ml-1 (Fig. S4c), signifying the internalization of BCP into the cells and the gradual release of PT2385. The fluorescence intensity remained constant, enabling the visualization of drug localization. Determine the absorption rate of BCP by chondrocytes. The results reveal that the absorption efficiency of the BCP drug delivery system by chondrocytes was 15.05 % after 1 h, 38.46 % after 6 h, and 72.30 % after 24 h. (Fig. S5b) Staining of the cell cytoskeleton confirmed the uptake of BCP by the cells, with pronounced fluorescence observed after 6 h and sustained stability noted after 24 h (Fig. 6 a). This stability endured for 48 h (Fig. S5a), likely attributed to the larger particle size of BCP compared to that of m-CQDs (Guo et al., 2022). The long-term and stable fluorescence indicated the drug carrier system's effective dissociation and sustained release of drugs within the cellular environment.

To assess the protective effects of the BCP drug carrier system on chondrocytes, we exposed the cells to an inflammatory environment and introduced BCP, m-CQDs, and PT2385 at the identical concentration (10 μg·ml-1). RTCA results demonstrated that BCP exhibited a protective effect equivalent to that of PT2385, effectively preserving chondrocytes in an inflammatory environment (Fig. 6b, Fig. S5 c). RT-qPCR and western blot analyses were conducted to evaluate the impact of BCP on HIF-2α and its downstream factors. The results indicated that BCP exhibited an inhibitory effect comparable to that of PT2385 at the cellular level, leading to the degradation of HIF-2α protein and a reduction in the expression of ADAMTS4 and MMPS (Fig. 6d). There was no significant difference in the inhibitory effects of BCP and PT2385 on HIF-2α and its downstream factors (Fig. S5d). These results indicate that BCP can effectively achieve the same therapeutic effect as PT2385 by sustaining the release of the drug into chondrocytes.
Depth of BCP penetration and its inhibition of HIF-2α in cartilage explant.

The extracellular culture environment of chondrocytes typically involves monolayer culture. Given its small particle size, PT2385 can readily enter chondrocytes and exert its effects through diffusion after being added to the culture medium. However, determining whether PT2385 can penetrate the barrier between joint fluid and the superficial layer of cartilage in the complex environment of cartilage tissue, effectively reaching deep within the cartilage tissue, requires further investigation. Existing research suggests that drugs smaller than 15 nm can penetrate the entire thickness of cartilage (Brown et al., 2019), whereas those with larger particle sizes may only reach the superficial layer. Small-molecule drugs (<1 nm) and large molecules (>7.5 nm) tend to diffuse primarily within the superficial zone of the cartilage, while medium-sized molecules diffuse most rapidly into the middle and deep zones (Leddy et al., 2003, Bajpayee et al., 2014). This phenomenon is attributed to the dense cartilage matrix, which acts as a significant barrier to the penetration of small-molecule drugs (Bajpayee and Grodzinsky, 2017).

Hence, we conducted experiments using healthy pig cartilage explants to compare the effects of PT2385 and BCP on the cartilage tissue. Initially, we evaluated the penetration depths of m-CQDs and BCP in healthy pig cartilage tissue. We observed that m-CQDs primarily accumulated on the surface of the upper layers of the cartilage tissue, whereas BCP was distributed throughout the entire cartilage layer, exhibiting significant presence in the middle to lower layers of the tissue (Fig. 7 b, Fig. S6). This difference may be attributed to the smaller particle size of the m-CQDs and the varying pore sizes between the different layers of the glycosaminoglycan (GAG) framework of the cartilage. Consequently, this finding suggests that BCP enabling successful penetration and deposition in the middle and lower layers of the cartilage tissue. Remarkably, these layers constitute the primary site of HIF-2α expression; thus, the appropriate deposition position allows BCP to effectively suppress HIF-2α expression in the deeper layers of the cartilage.

Subsequently, we conducted immunofluorescence analysis to assess the expression of HIF-2α and MMP13, the downstream factors of HIF-2α responsible for cartilage matrix degradation, in the cartilage explants. The results revealed that PT2385 significantly suppressed the expression of HIF-2α and MMP13 in the surface layer. However, the inhibitory effect in the middle and deep layers was notably weaker when compared to BCP. BCP demonstrated a robust inhibition of HIF-2α and MMP13 expression, spanning from the surface layer to the deep layers. HIF-2α expression was nearly undetectable in the deeper layers, and MMP13 expression was significantly reduced (Fig. 7c-d, Fig. S7-8). These findings underscore that through the modification of PT2385 to optimize its charge characteristics and particle size, BCP exhibits superior efficacy in the cartilage tissue compared to PT2385 alone.

ce:para.ce:bold: Intra-articular injection of BCP delays early cartilage degeneration

ce:section.ce:para: Given the encouraging outcomes from BCP in cell and cartilage explant experiments, its effectiveness in inhibiting early cartilage degeneration in animal models was assessed. Initially, we investigated the distribution location and metabolic cycle of BCP in mouse joints. Following the injection of BCP into the articular cavity, a substantial BCP fluorescence signal was observed in the mouse joint cartilage after 1 day, surpassing the intensity observed at 12 h. Crucially, the predominant location of BCP fluorescence was within the cartilage layer, indicating the effective penetration of the drug delivery system into the cartilage. BCP fluorescence gradually diminished within 3 to 5 days and was entirely absent after 7 days of injection (Fig. S9), signifying the complete metabolism of BCP in the joint cavity. Consequently, we anticipate that intra-articular BCP injections should be administered once every week, doubling the injection cycle of PT2385 (administered once every 3 days). The formulation of BCP allows for a reduced frequency of drug injections and ensures a more gradual drug release corresponding to the progression of OA.

For mid-to-late stage osteoarthritis (OA), joint replacement stands as the sole efficacious treatment option (Armiento et al., 2019). Hence, the timing of gene therapy selection is crucial, advocating for prompt interventions in the early stages of OA (Jones et al., 2019). To assess the efficacy of BCP in mitigating early-stage OA, we established a mouse model with mild severity. Intra-articular injections of PT2385, m-CQDs, and BCP were administered 2 weeks after destabilization of the medial meniscus (DMM) surgery, and the knee joints were examined 4 weeks later. The in vivo safety of PT2385, m-CQDs, and BCP was evaluated in the heart, liver, and kidney tissues of mice, revealing no significant myocardial degeneration, liver fibrosis, or pathological changes in the kidneys (Fig. S10a). Biochemical testing of serum samples from each group of mice showed no statistically significant differences in biochemical metabolism compared to the sham group (Fig. S10b). These experiments affirmed the safety of intra-articular injection of PT2385, m-CQDs, and BCP, indicating their lack of systemic organ toxicity or induction of blood metabolic disorders.

Subsequently, we assessed the impact of PT2385 and BCP on early-stage osteoarthritis (OA). X-ray examination revealed significant subchondral bone sclerosis in the vehicle and m-CQDs groups compared to the sham group, confirming the successful establishment of an early-stage OA model (Weinans et al., 2012, Gregson et al., 2017) (Fig. 8 a). However, intra-articular injections of PT2385 and BCP delayed subchondral bone sclerosis, indicating their protective effects on the joint cavity. Histological staining of the cartilage tissue using Safranin O-Fast Green and Alcian Blue disclosed healthier cartilage surfaces with a more uniform distribution of proteoglycans in the cartilage matrix for the PT2385 and BCP groups (Fig. 8b). The OARSI scores for these two groups were lower than those of the m-CQD and vehicle groups, signifying a positive impact on early-stage cartilage degeneration for PT2385 and BCP (Fig. S11a). However, no statistically significant differences between the two groups were observed, potentially due to the study being conducted at an early stage of OA, where changes in tissue structure, such as collagen and proteoglycans, were not yet detectable by histological staining.

Immunohistochemical findings revealed that both PT2385 and BCP effectively inhibited the expression of HIF-2α, along with downstream factors involved in cartilage degradation, including ADAMTS4 and MMP13. Notably, the inhibitory effect of BCP was more pronounced than that of PT2385 (Fig. 8c). Following the injection of PT2385 alone, HIF-2α expression persisted in the lower layers of the cartilage, whereas in the BCP group, HIF-2α expression throughout the entire cartilage layer was eliminated, accompanied by decreased expression of ADAMTS4 and MMP13 (Fig. S11b). It is essential to highlight that COL-II is not a direct downstream factor of Hif-2α, and significant changes in COL-II expression were not observed in cell experiments using PT2385. Moreover, the rapid loss of COL-II during OA contributes to metabolic disorders in the cartilage matrix, exacerbating OA progression. In the animal experiments, after intra-articular injection of PT2385 and BCP, the COL-II content in the cartilage tissue significantly exceeded that in the vehicle and m-CQD groups, with the BCP group exhibiting a higher Col-II content than the PT2385 group (Fig. 8c). This outcome indicates the superiority of BCP over PT2385, as efficient inhibition of HIF-2α delays the degradation of the cartilage matrix, achieving comprehensive cartilage protection.

To our knowledge, this study represents the first application of an m-CQD-targeted delivery system for the treatment of cartilage degeneration. In our study, we successfully devised a nano-delivery system for PT2385 that effectively and safely inhibited HIF-2α, thereby delaying the early progression of osteoarthritis (OA). Through the encapsulation of PT2385-loaded m-CQDs in bovine serum albumin (BSA), our delivery system exhibited pH-responsive characteristics, facilitating drug release in response to the extent of cartilage injury. This feature is crucial for enhancing drug delivery to the articular cavity. Furthermore, the construction of this delivery system addressed inherent challenges associated with the injection of small-molecule drugs into the articular cavity, such as limited penetration throughout the cartilage layer and rapid clearance rates in the articular cavity. In vivo experimental findings revealed that BCP not only suppressed the expression of Hif-2α and its downstream catabolic factors but also preserved Col-II in cartilage, shifting the balance towards positive regulation of the cartilage matrix. These results provide additional insights into a novel role of HIF-2α in cartilage tissue. In conclusion, BCP demonstrated a positive impact on the protection and repair of cartilage. We successfully devised a delivery system for PT2385 targeting articular cartilage, presenting a promising avenue for clinical applications as an innovative gene therapy strategy in the treatment of osteoarthritis (OA).

ce:para.ce:bold: Ethics approval and consent to participate.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: All animal experimental protocol has been carried out in accordance with relevant guidelines and all methods are reported in accordance with ARRIVE guidelines (https://arriveguidelines.org). All animal care and experimental procedures were conducted in strict adherence to the guidelines of the Experimental Animal Management Institution of Shanxi Medical University (Approval No: DW2022063).

Human cartilage tissue samples were obtained from the tibial plateaus of patients who underwent knee replacement surgery at the Second Hospital of Shanxi Medical University. All patients provided written informed consent to participate in the study.
The clinical study was conducted in strict adherence to the guidelines set forth by the Ethics Committee of the Second Hospital of Shanxi Medical University (Approval No. 2022.YX.No.167).
All data generated or analysed during this study are included in this article. Further enquiries can be directed to the corresponding author.
